title: Founder Readiness Radar

introduction.subtitle: Maturity assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Founder Readiness Radar helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Opportunity Insight, Execution Discipline, Resourcefulness & Economics, Resilience & Sustainability. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How strong is your evidence that the target problem is urgent?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Opportunity Insight: How strong is your evidence that the target problem is urgent
conceptHelp.summary: Opportunity Insight means evidence that a real customer problem is urgent, painful, frequent, and worth solving now. This question narrows the concept to "How strong is your evidence that the target problem is urgent?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Opportunity Insight matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How strong is your evidence that the target problem is urgent?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How precisely can you define the first customer segment and buying trigger?

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.
B. There is a high-level description, but segmentation, trigger, or buyer evidence is thin.
C. The answer is defined for a specific segment with documented behaviour, alternatives, and decision criteria.
D. The answer is validated across repeated cases and directly informs targeting, messaging, prioritisation, and trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do you choose the most important outcome for the coming week?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Execution Discipline; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Execution Discipline, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Execution Discipline has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Execution Discipline is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Execution Discipline: How do you choose the most important outcome for the coming week
conceptHelp.summary: Execution Discipline means the habit of choosing outcomes, shipping useful work, learning from it, and keeping promises visible. This question narrows the concept to "How do you choose the most important outcome for the coming week?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Execution Discipline matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How do you choose the most important outcome for the coming week?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How frequently do you ship something that can change a decision?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: What happens when a critical resource is missing?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Resourcefulness & Economics; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.

Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.summary: Resourcefulness & Economics means the ability to make progress with scarce resources while understanding runway, unit economics, and trade-offs. This question narrows the concept to "What happens when a critical resource is missing?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

questions.text: How well do you understand runway and the economics of the next milestone?

options.text: A. There is no usable model; the answer is unknown or based on rough intuition.
B. There is an estimate, but assumptions, sensitivity, and ownership are weak.
C. The model uses current inputs, documented assumptions, and regular review.
D. The model is scenario-tested, tied to triggers and decisions, and reconciled with reliable source data.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Resourcefulness & Economics: How well do you understand runway and the economics of the next milestone

questions.text: How do you react to evidence that contradicts your preferred idea?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.

Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Resilience & Sustainability: How do you react to evidence that contradicts your preferred idea
conceptHelp.summary: Resilience & Sustainability means how the founder responds to contradictory evidence, pressure, fatigue, and the risk of building in an unsustainable way. This question narrows the concept to "How do you react to evidence that contradicts your preferred idea?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

questions.text: What evidence shows the first segment is actively trying to solve the problem now?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows current problem-solving behaviour in the first segment; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of current problem-solving behaviour in the first segment, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show current problem-solving behaviour in the first segment in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Current problem-solving behaviour in the first segment is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Opportunity Insight: What evidence shows the first segment is actively trying to solve the problem now
conceptHelp.summary: Opportunity Insight is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What evidence shows the first segment is actively trying to solve the problem now?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Opportunity Insight matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
